title: Computational Paper Guidelines
text:
NoteData available in an accessible permanent repository (DOI)






Zenodo
other institutional repositories (find examples)










NoteFree license (e.g., Creative Commons)





The License must be clearly indicated in the root of the repository (LICENSE/COPYING).
Exemple of valid licenses:

CC-BY-4.0 — Attribution; for data/docs; FAIR-compliant openness.
refine possibilities in CC-BY










NoteFile listing authors and contributors





The authors and contributors should be given with one or several of the following:

AUTHORS file — Plain text or Markdown file listing main authors and maintainers. Example: AUTHORS, AUTHORS.md
CONTRIBUTORS file — Lists additional contributors or institutional credits. Example: CONTRIBUTORS, CONTRIBUTORS.md
Within README file — Optional “Authors” or “Credits” section; human-readable summary.
Within Version control management — With clean commit history, the exact contributor list is metadata.

add a warning regarding usability with heavy data














NoteOpen file formats






e.g., HDF5, NetCDF
find a website with list of acceptable things










Notemetadata file






metadata extracted from file scanning (Solidipes, other examples, …)
Open file formats
RO-crate format for storage of metadata










NoteDocumentation





For complex and/or voluminous datasets documentation is necessary












NoteManuscript/dataset concordance





Curation and validation ensuring consistency between content and paper

Can be human validation
Ontology based validation (using Solidipes)

title: Computational Paper Guidelines
text:
FAIR Software

Level 1






TipCode hosted on a forge with version, commit number or Zenodo DOI






git forges:

GitLab — Open-core; self-host or cloud; good CI/CD; FAIR-aligned transparency.
GitHub — Widely used; strong integration tools; limited openness (proprietary).
Codeberg — Fully open-source (Gitea-based); privacy-friendly; EU-hosted.
SourceHut — Minimalist; open protocols; plain-text workflows; fully libre.
Bitbucket — Proprietary; integrates with Atlassian suite; less open.
Gitea — Lightweight, self-hosted, MIT-licensed; supports FAIR openness.
Forgejo — Gitea fork; community-driven; strong open governance.
GRICAD GitLab — Academic research-forge (Université Grenoble Alpes + CNRS + INRIA); local GitLab instance for research software. - IRD Forge — Institutional GitLab-based forge by Institut de recherche pour le développement (IRD); supports versioning, CI/CD, public/private code. :contentReferenceoaicite:2
Forgejo / Fordj (France-specific interest) — Libre community-forge (via DINUM / BlueHats) for French public sector; open-source development. :contentReferenceoaicite:4



Getting version / git commit:

Git commit
GitHub commit
Gilab commit
Zenodo DOI, code archive



Citation

README file: Doe, J., & Smith, A. (2025). CoolSim: Climate simulation toolkit (Version 2.3.1) [Computer software]. GitHub. Commit: 4f8a9d2. https://github.com/exampleuser/coolsim/tree/4f8a9d2
As a bibtex entry:

@software{Doe_CoolSim_2025,
  author       = {Doe, John and Smith, Alice},
  title        = {CoolSim: Climate simulation toolkit},
  year         = {2025},
  version      = {2.3.1},
  url          = {https://github.com/exampleuser/coolsim/tree/4f8a9d2},
  note         = {Commit 4f8a9d2b6e7c4e1a52d9e9e2f3c17b6a1d3f45ac},
}










TipFree license





The License must be clearly indicated in the root of the repository (LICENSE/COPYING). Exemple of valid licenses:

MIT License — Permissive; free reuse, modify, distribute; attribution only.
Apache 2.0 — Permissive; attribution + patent grant; high interoperability.
BSD 3-Clause — Permissive; attribution + non-endorsement.
GPL-3.0 — Copyleft; derivatives must remain open.
LGPL-3.0 — Copyleft; Lesser GPL permits use of the library in proprietary programs.
MPL-2.0 — Weak copyleft; file-level openness; flexible reuse.
CC-BY-4.0 — Attribution; for data/docs; FAIR-compliant openness.










TipAuthors and contributors file





The authors and contributors should be given with one or several of the following:

AUTHORS file — Plain text or Markdown file listing main authors and maintainers. Example: AUTHORS, AUTHORS.md
CONTRIBUTORS file — Lists additional contributors or institutional credits. Example: CONTRIBUTORS, CONTRIBUTORS.md
Within README file — Optional “Authors” or “Credits” section; human-readable summary.
Within Version control management — With clean commit history, the exact contributor list is metadata.






Level 2






TipDocumented code





There is API documentation (inline annotations) and a user/developper documentation.

Doxygen
Sphinx and pydoc
JSDoc
Documenter.jl
rustdoc and Cargo doc










Tip“How to use it?”






instructions
README
example scripts










TipMetadata file





TODO





Level 3






TipExamples















TipAutomated tests















TipFunctionality documentation (e.g., API documentation)

title: Computational Paper Guidelines
text:
Reproducible Results

Level 1






CautionReproducibility recipe






README with instructions
Input accessible
Script for relaunching raw generation and post-analysis










CautionPersistent identifier






GIT Repository
SWHID
DOI






Level 2






CautionFull hardware/software environment






Guix - a functional, reproducible, and transparent package manager. Docs
venv - is a Python library for isolated environments. Docs
requirements.txt - standard pip list of packages. Docs

environment.yml - Conda environment specification for Python + packages. Docs

package.json - main metadata + dependencies Docs

renv - is a project-managed R environment for reproducible research. Docs










CautionWorkflow Managers






Nextflow

📌 https://www.nextflow.io
📦 GitHub: https://github.com/nextflow-io/nextflow
✅ Advanced: DSL2, containers, cloud support, bioinformatics focus.

Snakemake

📌 https://snakemake.readthedocs.io
📦 GitHub: https://github.com/snakemake/snakemake
✅ Python-based, rule-based, great for reproducibility and pipelines.

CWL (Common Workflow Language)

📌 https://www.commonwl.org
📦 GitHub: https://github.com/common-workflow-language/common-workflow-language
✅ Language-agnostic, portable, cloud-native.

WDL (Workflow Description Language)

📌 https://github.com/broadinstitute/wdl
📦 GitHub: https://github.com/broadinstitute/wdl
✅ Developed by Broad Institute, used in Terra, GA4GH.

Airflow (Apache Airflow)

📌 https://airflow.apache.org
📦 GitHub: https://github.com/apache/airflow
✅ Orchestration of complex data pipelines (not just science workflows).

Galaxy (Tool/Workflow Platform)

📌 https://galaxyproject.org
📦 GitHub: https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy
✅ GUI-driven, open, widely used in bioinformatics.
